oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Crazy for Ringneck Parakeets
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Jordan, Rick
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 50 Number 3 (July 2023).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.Crazy for Ringneck ParakeetsThe ringneck or Rose-ringed parakeets of India have become a huge aviary success in the past few decades. There are two wild forms recognized in U.S. aviaries, the nominate from N. Africa (Psittacula krameri krameri) and the subspecies from India (Psittacula krameri manillensis). African ringneck parakeets are now quite hard to find and only available in the natural wild green form, whereas the Indian form is now available in over 30 different color mutations and is widely bred across the world. It is doubtful that many are being captured and taken from the wild at this point in history since they are so easily bred in captivity.Back in the 1960’s, Indian ringnecks were only available in the wild-type or color green here in the United States. It was not until the 70’s that the first lutino (yellow with red eyes) mutation was bred, and it demanded a very high price. In fact, the first lutino ringnecks in the U.S. might have been imported from breeders in another country, the facts are not easily substantiated at this point. Suffice to say, the first sales of lutino ringnecks in the U.S. were very high, a seasoned breeder compared their price to that of a Major Mitchell’s cockatoo at that time.Click the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 50 No. 3 (2023): Watchbird July 2023; 4-6
